content:
Every developer who has joined an existing project knows the feeling.
You clone the repository. You open the codebase. And you start reading, not to understand the features, but to understand the intentions.
Why is this service structured this way? Why does this module import directly from infrastructure? Why are there two implementations of what looks like the same thing?
The first place you look is the README. If you’re lucky, it tells you how to run the project locally. If you’re very lucky, there’s an architecture section. Usually it was written two years ago and describes a system that no longer quite exists.
So you open Confluence. Dozens of pages, some of them detailed, most of them dated. The further back you go, the more accurate they are, but the less relevant. The recent pages are stubs. Half of them say “TODO: update this.”
So you ask the team. The senior developers are helpful, but they joined after some of the key decisions were made. The tech lead knows the what but not always the why. The architect who designed the original system moved to another company eighteen months ago.
Eventually you piece it together. The code is the most reliable source of truth, not because it explains itself, but because it’s the only thing that hasn’t drifted from reality.
This is brownfield development. And it’s where most of us spend most of our time.
In the previous article, we covered greenfield: how to structure specs and context files before the first line of code exists. That’s the ideal scenario. This article is about the common one.
Here’s the paradox: In greenfield, you teach AI your standards.
In brownfield, you and AI have to reconstruct standards together, from code that was never written to be explained.
Why Brownfield Is Harder for AI Than It Looks
When you ask AI to help with an existing codebase without context, something subtle happens.
AI doesn’t see a codebase with gaps and inconsistencies. It sees patterns. And it completes them.
If the codebase has business logic scattered across controllers and services without a clear boundary, AI will continue that pattern, because it looks like the established convention. If there are interfaces everywhere, with or without justification, AI will generate more interfaces. If error handling is inconsistent across modules, AI will pick one of the existing approaches and apply it, whichever one appears most frequently, or most recently.
This is the core brownfield problem. AI isn’t wrong to follow the patterns it sees. But some of those patterns are accidents, not decisions. Some are technical debt that was never addressed. Some are violations of the intended architecture that survived because no one had time to fix them.
Without context, AI can’t distinguish between a pattern that was chosen deliberately and one that happened by drift. It treats both as equally valid guidance.
The result is that AI doesn’t just continue your codebase, it amplifies whatever is already there. Good patterns and bad patterns alike, at the same speed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is why a context file matters even more in brownfield than in greenfield. In greenfield, it prevents drift from starting. In brownfield, it prevents AI from accelerating drift that’s already in motion.
Phase 1: Reverse Engineering the Context
Before you can write a context file, you need to understand what you’re working with.
In brownfield, this means doing something AI can help you with but can’t do alone: reconstructing intent from code.
Reading the code as archaeology
The first step is treating the codebase as an archaeological site. Every layer of code represents a decision made at a specific point in time, with specific constraints that may no longer exist. Your job is to distinguish between what was intended and what accumulated.
Some questions to guide this reading:
What are the layers, and are they respected?
Look at the import graph. Which modules import from which? If the architecture was supposed to be layered, domain, application, infrastructure, does the code actually respect those boundaries, or do you find domain logic importing from infrastructure, controllers with business rules, repositories with application orchestration?
What’s consistent, and what’s inconsistent?
Consistency is a signal of deliberate decision. Inconsistency is a signal of drift, or of multiple developers with different standards working without coordination, or of architectural evolution that was never completed.
Where is the debt concentrated?
Almost every brownfield codebase has areas that everyone avoids. The module nobody wants to touch. The service that works but nobody understands. These areas are where the original context has been most completely lost, and where AI is most dangerous without guidance.
What decisions are irreversible?
Some violations of intended architecture can be corrected incrementally. Others are so deeply embedded in the data model, the API contracts, or the deployment topology that correcting them would require a rewrite. Knowing which is which shapes what you put in the context file.
Using AI to help with the archaeology
This is one of the places where AI is genuinely useful in brownfield work, not to generate code, but to analyze it.
Give AI a module or a set of files and ask it to describe what it sees: what patterns it detects, what the implied architectural intent appears to be, where it notices inconsistencies. Ask it to identify what a reasonable architecture for this codebase might look like, based on what it can read.
This isn’t a substitute for your own reading. But it surfaces things quickly, naming patterns you might have noticed intuitively but not yet articulated, flagging inconsistencies you haven’t reached yet, identifying where the architecture diverges from common conventions.
Treat AI’s analysis as a first draft of understanding, not a conclusion. It doesn’t know which inconsistencies are deliberate exceptions and which are accidents. You do, or you’ll learn to, as you go deeper.
Phase 2: Building a CLAUDE.md for a System You Didn’t Create
Once you have enough understanding to articulate what the system is, and what it was probably intended to be, you can start building the context file.
The brownfield context file has a different character than the greenfield one. In greenfield, it describes a system you designed. In brownfield, it describes a system you’re interpreting, and it needs to be honest about that distinction.
Start with what you know is true
The first section of a brownfield context file should describe what you’ve verified, not what you’ve inferred. Stick to things you can point to in the code:
“The backend follows a layered structure with domain, application, and infrastructure packages. The boundary is mostly respected, there are known violations in the payments module which are documented below.”
Note the honesty: “mostly respected” and “known violations.” This matters because AI will follow your context file. If you describe the architecture as cleaner than it is, AI will generate code that’s inconsistent with the actual codebase it’s working in.
Document the violations explicitly
This is the part most people skip, and it’s the most important part of a brownfield context file.
For every known architectural violation, every place where the code doesn’t match the intended design, document it. Name it. Explain why it exists if you know. And state clearly whether it’s a target for correction or a permanent exception.
A section like this:
## Known Violations
### Payments module - direct infrastructure import in domain
Location: src/domain/services/payment-validator.ts
Violation: imports from infrastructure/stripe directly
Why it exists: added under time pressure before the port/adapter pattern was established
Status: TARGET FOR CORRECTION - do not replicate this pattern
When working in payments: follow the intended pattern (port in domain, adapter in infrastructure),
even though existing code doesn'tThis section does something critical: it tells AI which patterns to follow and which to avoid, even when the codebase presents both. Without it, AI treats violations as valid examples and continues them.
Describe the intended architecture, not the actual one
The Constitution layer of a brownfield context file describes where the system is supposed to be, not necessarily where it is. This is deliberate.
If the intended architecture is hexagonal but the current codebase has fifteen percent of the business logic in controllers, the Constitution still describes hexagonal architecture. The violations section documents the gap. And the context file is explicit: new code follows the intended architecture; violations are corrected incrementally, not replicated.
This gives AI a clear directive: when generating new code or modifying existing code, follow the intent, not the accidents.
Be explicit about what AI should never assume
One section that brownfield context files need and greenfield ones don’t:
a list of things AI should never infer from existing patterns without asking.
In a greenfield codebase, patterns are deliberate by definition, you established them. In brownfield, some patterns are deliberate and some are accidents. AI can’t tell the difference. So you tell it explicitly:
## Do Not Infer From Existing Code
- Do not assume that because a controller handles business logic in one module,
that pattern is acceptable elsewhere. All business logic belongs in use cases.
- Do not assume that because some services are instantiated with `new`,
that approach is preferred. All service dependencies must be injected.
- Do not assume that because error handling is inconsistent across modules,
that inconsistency is acceptable. Follow the error hierarchy defined below.
- When in doubt, ask - do not default to the most common existing pattern.This section reframes AI’s default behavior. Instead of “follow what you see most often,” the directive becomes “follow what this file says, and flag when existing code contradicts it.”
Phase 3: Handling Violations Already in Production
This is the most difficult part of brownfield context engineering, because it’s not just a documentation problem, it’s a strategy problem.
You have violations in production code. They’re real. They’re running. Fixing them has risk. Not fixing them means AI will see them, and without clear guidance, will continue them.
The three categories of existing violations
Not all violations are equal, and treating them equally leads to either over-engineering the correction or under-managing the risk.
Category 1: Safe to correct incrementally.
These are violations where the fix is localized, it doesn’t affect the API contract, the data model, or other modules. Business logic in a controller that can be extracted to a use case. A direct database call in a service that can be wrapped in a repository. These go into the context file as targets for active correction: when AI touches code in this area, it should correct the violation as part of the change.
Category 2: Requires planned migration.
These are violations that are load-bearing, fixing them requires changes across multiple layers, coordinated releases, or data migrations. They can’t be corrected incidentally. They go into the context file as documented debt with a migration plan: AI should not replicate the pattern, and should flag when new code would depend on the violation.
Category 3: Permanent exceptions.
Some violations aren’t actually violations in context, they’re places where the general rule doesn’t apply for reasons that are specific and defensible. These go into the context file as explicit exceptions: AI should follow this pattern in this specific area, and here’s why.
The worst outcome is treating Category 2 and 3 as Category 1, attempting to correct load-bearing violations incrementally without a plan, and breaking something in production. The second worst is treating Category 1 as Cat
